title: Retreat, Rebel, Replace, or Reform? Making Sense of Multilateralism Under Trump 2.0
gist: Carnegie argues the conventional view that Trump 2.0 simply repudiates multilateralism is wrong; its conduct splits into four overlapping modes (disengagement, coercion/disruption, substitution, conditional continuity), and the cumulative effect is an erosion of U.S. credibility and influence that hands openings above all to China, even as some forced institutional reform may be a silver lining.

insights.claim: The Trump administration's behavior toward international organizations can be sorted into four overlapping categories that the authors label retreat (disengagement), rebellion (coercion/disruption), replacement (substitution), and reform (conditional continuity), and these are pursued in parallel even within the same institution.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Gives investors a structured lens to map U.S. posture institution-by-institution rather than assuming blanket exit, which mispriced risk for bodies the U.S. is actually deepening engagement with.
insights.quote: disengagement, disruption, substitution, and conditional continuity. Or, for the more alliteratively minded: retreat, rebellion, replacement, and reform.
insights.locator: The administration’s behaviors toward multilateralism

insights.claim: The authors contend the administration's animating impulses are sovereigntist, transactional, ideological, and leverage-focused, producing a selective and ideologically conditioned engagement rather than pure hostility or wholesale exit.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Signals U.S. moves are predictable along these axes: watch for sovereignty triggers, ideological flashpoints (climate, gender), and where the U.S. holds financial leverage.
insights.quote: the administration’s animating impulses have been sovereigntist, transactional, ideological, and leverage focused.
insights.locator: In sum, the administration’s animating impulses

insights.claim: The authors argue the U.S. has always been ambivalent and selective toward multilateralism, so Trump differs from predecessors in the intensity of his antipathy and appetite for disruption rather than in kind on most fronts.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Frames Trump as an acceleration of a structural U.S. ambivalence, suggesting reversion risk after political turnover but also that disruption is path-dependent, not purely personal.
insights.quote: What sets the Trump administration apart from its predecessors is the intensity of its antipathy.
insights.locator: What sets the Trump administration apart

insights.claim: On inauguration day Trump initiated withdrawal from the Paris Climate Agreement and the WHO and pulled the U.S. out of the OECD global minimum corporate tax agreement, framing the tax deal as a sovereignty infringement and the climate accord as a rip-off.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: OECD pillar-two exit reshapes multinational tax planning; Paris/WHO exits reroute global climate and health funding flows away from U.S. influence.
insights.quote: He also pulled the United States out of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development’s global minimum corporate tax agreement.
insights.locator: On inauguration day, Trump signed executive orders

insights.claim: The U.S. WHO withdrawal completed January 22, 2026 stripped the body of its largest donor (about $1.284 billion per year), prompting the WHO to cut roughly a quarter of its workforce and consolidate ten divisions into four.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A diminished WHO weakens global pandemic surveillance, a tail risk for global supply chains, travel, and pharma; watch capacity gaps and successor arrangements.
insights.quote: the WHO has initiated plans to slash roughly one-quarter of its workforce and consolidate its ten divisions into four.
insights.locator: The administration justified its WHO withdrawal

insights.claim: China is set to replace the U.S. as the WHO's top state donor, pledging an additional $500 million over five years—roughly a forty-fold increase over its prior $2.5 million annual voluntary contribution—which entrenches the very Chinese influence the administration feared.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Concrete proof that U.S. retreat directly hands soft-power and standard-setting leverage to Beijing at low cost; a template for other vacated bodies.
insights.quote: China, which is now set to replace the United States as the WHO’s top state donor, has pledged an additional $500 million to the organization over the next five years
insights.locator: The administration justified its WHO withdrawal

insights.claim: A January 7, 2026 presidential memorandum directed U.S. withdrawal from 31 UN entities and 35 non-UN intergovernmental bodies, but the cull spared the World Bank, IMF, and the UN itself, defying the most extreme Project 2025 recommendations.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Bretton Woods institutions remain anchored to U.S. participation despite Project 2025 pressure, reducing tail risk to the dollar-centric financial architecture, but the ongoing review keeps that risk live.
insights.quote: It directed U.S. withdrawal from thirty-one UN entities and thirty-five non-UN intergovernmental bodies
insights.locator: On January 7, 2026, the White House released

insights.claim: The authors warn the January 7 withdrawals may be the zenith of disengagement, but it is equally plausible the administration is simply taking more time before moving against larger, more consequential organizations.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Keep tail risk priced for further U.S. exits from major IOs given Rubio's ongoing review; the high-water mark is not confirmed.
insights.quote: The January 7 withdrawals could turn out to be the zenith of U.S. disengagement.
insights.locator: Still, the culling could have been far more dramatic.

insights.claim: The administration's most striking disengagement is its dismissal of international legal restraints on the use of force, with the authors calling its disdain a difference in kind, not merely degree, citing the war on Iran, extrajudicial killing of suspected narcotics traffickers, and threats to seize Greenland.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Erosion of the use-of-force prohibition raises geopolitical risk premia broadly and signals reduced predictability for sovereign and commodity exposures near conflict zones.
insights.quote: The Trump administration has been a difference in kind, not merely degree, displaying a blatant disdain for any international legal constraints on U.S. power.
insights.locator: To be sure, the U.S. relationship toward international law

insights.claim: The authors argue the claim that multilateralism endangers U.S. sovereignty is a canard, because joining an IO pursuant to the Constitution is an exercise rather than abrogation of sovereignty and the U.S. never accedes to supranational hierarchy as EU members do.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Reframes the sovereignty argument as rhetoric, implying many withdrawals are ideological rather than cost-benefit driven and therefore potentially reversible.
insights.quote: the notion that multilateralism endangers U.S. sovereignty is a canard.
insights.locator: A repeated refrain in the Trump administration’s repudiation

themes.theme: Coercion: UN dues, tariffs, and the trade order

insights.claim: The U.S. has refused to pay the vast majority of its assessed UN dues, accumulating roughly $4 billion in arrears as of spring 2026, with the U.S. accounting for over 95 percent of overdue payments to the regular budget.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A funding cliff for the UN; Guterres warned of possible financial collapse as early as July 2026 absent dues payment or budget-rule overhaul.
insights.quote: resulting in a collective $4 billion in U.S. arrears to the world body as of spring 2026.
insights.locator: The most obvious coercive tool

insights.claim: Trump's FY26 budget sought to cut U.S. contributions to international organizations by 83 percent (from $1.54 billion to $263.8 million) and zero out UN peacekeeping assessments, while a House panel's FY27 bill would cut IO contributions a further 77.6 percent and zero out the UN regular budget.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Structural, multi-year defunding trajectory, not a one-off; signals durable financial pressure on the UN system regardless of any near-term partial payments.
insights.quote: Trump’s FY26 budget request called on Congress to slash U.S. contributions to international organizations by 83 percent
insights.locator: The most obvious coercive tool

insights.claim: The administration is withholding dues to force UN reform, with Ambassador Mike Waltz framing the goal as a UN that does less and does it better, and it released only about $160 million of the $4 billion owed while demanding nine quick-win reforms.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: The leverage is conditional and reform-linked; watch summer 2026 for a possible reform-for-dues breakthrough that would re-rate UN solvency risk.
insights.quote: a UN that “does less and does it better.”
insights.locator: There is some method to the Trump administration’s madness.

insights.claim: The nine UN quick-win reforms demanded include overhauling the pension system, ending business-class travel below undersecretary-general level, shifting from human interpreters to AI-powered translation, and blocking China from channeling tens of millions annually to a discretionary fund in the secretary-general's office.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Concrete reform checklist to monitor as conditions for U.S. payment; AI substitution and anti-China provisions show the ideological and tech dimensions of the bargaining.
insights.quote: shifting from human interpreters to AI-powered translation and interpretation tools, and blocking China from channeling tens of millions of dollars to a discretionary fund
insights.locator: In April, internal memos circulated

insights.claim: The authors cite the 1985 Kassebaum-Solomon Amendment and the 1999 Helms-Biden agreement as precedents showing U.S. financial coercion of the UN can produce negotiated, pro-U.S. reform outcomes when tied to clear demands and allied coordination.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Historical base rate suggests dues-withholding often ends in negotiated reform rather than collapse; current weakness is the lack of allied coordination.
insights.quote: Financial “coercion,” as these examples attest, can produce positive results.
insights.locator: Deploying financial leverage over the United Nations

insights.claim: The administration has wielded tariffs more aggressively and broadly than any predecessor, deploying them beyond Liberation Day to pressure migration/fentanyl flows, punish Brazil over Bolsonaro, threaten Europe over Greenland, and protect autos and pharma—so far failing to shrink the trade deficit.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Tariffs are a general-purpose coercion tool tied to non-trade objectives, raising unpredictability for cross-border supply chains and country risk far beyond trade balances.
insights.quote: imposing them on strategic competitors and allies alike to exert its will both in bilateral and multilateral settings.
insights.locator: Beyond withholding dues, of course, the administration

insights.claim: U.S. tariff threats successfully torpedoed the International Maritime Organization's net-zero shipping framework, with vulnerable Caribbean states (Antigua and Barbuda, the Bahamas, Haiti, Jamaica) flipping from support to abstention or deferral under threat of port fees, visa restrictions, and sanctions.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Shows U.S. coercion can reverse near-final binding multilateral deals; removes near-term carbon cost overhang on shipping but proves small-state votes are buyable, raising governance fragility.
insights.quote: The pressure worked. Some countries that had voted decisively in support of the measure only a few months earlier either abstained or voted to defer
insights.locator: A prime example is its campaign to torpedo

insights.claim: A February 2026 Supreme Court ruling cast doubt on the IEEPA legal basis for tariffs, potentially forcing a shift to slower Section 301 investigations, but the authors warn the coercive trade agenda is not spent given the administration's creativity in finding legal authorities.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Legal constraint may slow but not stop tariff actions; watch for migration to Section 301, which is more procedurally telegraphed and thus more predictable for markets.
insights.quote: the administration has demonstrated consistent creativity in finding legal authorities to support its preferred outcomes
insights.locator: The legal foundation for much of this mercantilist

insights.claim: The administration is substituting the WTO's nondiscrimination/MFN system with customized bilateral agreements on reciprocal trade (ARTs) that maximize U.S. leverage but outsource U.S. tariff policy to other countries, expose poor nations to higher tariffs, and risk fragmenting global trade.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A managed-trade regime increases administrative burden and trade diversion; durability is doubtful given fickle U.S. tariff commitments, complicating long-horizon supply-chain investment.
insights.quote: A policy of strict reciprocity on trade barriers in effect outsources U.S. tariff policy to other countries
insights.locator: Finally, the Trump administration has introduced substitution in its trade policy

insights.claim: U.S. blockage of WTO Appellate Body appointments represents bipartisan continuity rather than departure, having begun under Obama, escalated under Trump's first term in 2017, paralyzed the body below quorum in December 2019, and continued uninterrupted through Biden and Trump 2.0.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: WTO dispute settlement remains structurally broken across administrations, so do not price a near-term revival; trade disputes will keep migrating to bilateral coercion.
insights.quote: that policy of paralysis has continued uninterrupted through the Biden and second Trump terms.
insights.locator: The most obvious case is continued U.S. blockage

insights.claim: Under heavy U.S. pressure NATO agreed to a defense spending target of 5 percent of GDP by 2035, with Spain the only holdout, achieving burden-sharing that administrations of both parties had sought for decades.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A structural, decade-long tailwind for European defense spending and defense contractors; Spain as outlier flags fiscal-political friction.
insights.quote: the alliance finally agreed to a defense spending target of 5 percent of GDP by 2035—with Spain the only holdout
insights.locator: On security, the administration’s approach to NATO

insights.claim: Trump has treated NATO's Article 5 collective-defense guarantee as contingent on spending and has floated leaving NATO over allied reluctance on Iran, ordering withdrawal of 5,000 U.S. troops from Germany after its chancellor called the Iran war a humiliation for the U.S.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Conditioning Article 5 erodes deterrence credibility that money cannot rebuild, raising European security risk premia and accelerating strategic autonomy and hedging.
insights.quote: Treating Article 5 as a bargaining chip, however, risks jeopardizing something that increased allied spending cannot easily repair
insights.locator: But this recent U.S. achievement has come at heavy diplomatic cost.

insights.claim: The authors frame NATO as simultaneously better funded and more strategically uncertain than at any point in its history, calling how that tension resolves one of the most consequential open questions in international security.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Higher spending and lower credibility can coexist; the binary outcome (cohesion vs fracture) is a major macro/geopolitical swing factor for European assets.
insights.quote: NATO today is simultaneously better funded and more strategically uncertain than at any point in its history.
insights.locator: the foundational credibility of the alliance

insights.claim: From March 2025 the U.S. Mission to the UN announced it would oppose any resolution mentioning the Sustainable Development Goals, which the authors call patently absurd since the SDGs are voluntary and impose no binding legal obligation.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Signals U.S. will block ESG/development language across UN texts, complicating multilateral development-finance and sustainability agendas firms rely on.
insights.quote: it would oppose any UN resolution that mentioned the Sustainable Development Goals
insights.locator: In March 2025, the U.S. Mission to the UN announced

insights.claim: At the March 2026 UN Commission on the Status of Women the U.S. forced a vote on a normally consensus document and lost 37-1 with six abstentions, citing objections to gender ideology, reproductive-health language, and broad AI-regulation language.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
entities: UN Commission on the Status of Women
insights.so_what: Demonstrates U.S. isolation on social/AI-governance language; AI-regulation objections foreshadow U.S. resistance to multilateral AI rules.
insights.quote: the UN’s Economic and Social Council approved it by a vote of thirty-seven to one (with six abstentions), resulting in a standing ovation and derision at the U.S. stance.
insights.locator: The apotheosis of U.S. ideological grandstanding

insights.claim: The U.S. rejected the UN International Migration Review Forum declaration, with the State Department denouncing alleged replacement immigration, echoing Trump's September 2025 UN General Assembly claim that the UN is funding an assault on Western countries and their borders.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: U.S. migration politics now drive multilateral obstruction, raising friction in any global compact touching migration, labor mobility, or refugee response.
insights.quote: describing past multilateral migration assistance as having enabled “the invasion of our country” by “millions of foreigners from the worst corners of the world.”
insights.locator: A further illustration came in May

insights.claim: Despite U.S. pressure and boycott, the November 2025 G20 Johannesburg summit endorsed a 122-point declaration with only Argentina joining the U.S. in dissent, showing the limits of U.S. coercion when other members coordinate.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Collective resistance can defeat U.S. pressure, a counterweight signal for middle-power coordination and Global South agenda-setting.
insights.quote: the other members (with the exception of Argentina) endorsed a 122-point statement.
insights.locator: In the run-up to the November 2025 leaders’ level summit

insights.claim: The administration is building bespoke minilateral coalitions to bypass legacy IOs, with the authors coining Mar-a-Lago multilateralism for arrangements that are highly personalized, partisan, and transactional, distinguishing Trump from prior minilateral practice.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Investors should track parallel U.S.-led clubs as the real venues of power and rule-setting, not just legacy bodies, especially in tech and security.
insights.quote: Call it Mar-a-Lago multilateralism.
insights.locator: The charter’s most novel and jarring element

insights.claim: The Board of Peace, UN Security Council-authorized on November 17, 2025 for Gaza, gives Trump as a private individual the sole veto and the inaugural chairmanship (retainable after he leaves office), with a $1 billion minimum contribution for executive-committee membership subject to his personal approval.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A pay-to-play governance vehicle skewing toward autocracies; its expansive charter language positions it as a potential UN rival, a fragmentation risk to global peace architecture.
insights.quote: The charter sets a minimum contribution of $1 billion for heads of government to become permanent members of the board’s executive committee, subject again to Trump’s personal approval.
insights.locator: The charter’s most novel and jarring element

insights.claim: As of April only 27 of 58 invited nations had joined the Board of Peace, skewing toward autocratic and semi-autocratic governments while few Western allies joined, preferring observer status, and France's foreign minister publicly opposed any body replacing the UN.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Weak Western buy-in caps the Board's legitimacy and durability, suggesting it stays Gaza-focused rather than supplanting the UN near term.
insights.quote: membership skewing heavily toward autocratic and semi-autocratic governments
insights.locator: As of April, twenty-seven nations had joined

insights.claim: Trump unveiled the Shield of the Americas on March 7, 2026 at Doral with thirteen ideologically aligned leaders, pointedly excluding Brazil, Colombia, and Mexico (over half of Latin America's GDP) and Canada, to bypass the OAS and Summit of the Americas under a revived Monroe Doctrine.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A militarized, ideologically gated hemispheric bloc that the authors deem unsustainable across democratic turnover; exclusion of the largest economies limits its economic reach.
insights.quote: Pointedly absent were representatives from left-wing governments of Brazil, Colombia, and Mexico—which collectively account for more than half of Latin America’s GDP
insights.locator: Trump unveiled it on March 7, 2026

insights.claim: After leaving the WHO, HHS proposed roughly $2 billion to replicate disease surveillance and pandemic response bilaterally—about three times the prior annual WHO cost—while leaving coverage gaps such as data Russia and China provide to the WHO.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Substitution costs more and covers less, a textbook negative cost-benefit; degraded surveillance is a latent pandemic and biosecurity risk for markets.
insights.quote: Beyond costing three times as much as what the United States previously spent annually on the WHO, such an arrangement would still leave gaping holes in global coverage
insights.locator: Following the formal U.S. departure from the WHO

insights.claim: Pax Silica, launched in 2025, is a U.S.-led coalition to set shared standards, investment partnerships, and supply-chain arrangements for semiconductors and advanced technologies, reflecting a determination to compete with China across technology stacks via allies controlling key chokepoints.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A new allied chip-governance bloc to watch alongside export controls; membership and chokepoint coverage will shape semiconductor capex and trade flows.
insights.quote: Pax Silica, launched in 2025, may be the most ambitious.
insights.locator: The second Trump administration has similarly originated

insights.claim: At the February 2026 Critical Minerals Ministerial the U.S. launched FORGE (Forum on Resource Geostrategic Engagement), a successor to the Biden-era Minerals Security Partnership designed as a preferential trade-and-investment zone using coordinated price floors to counter Chinese market manipulation, aiming to link bilateral MOUs into a plurilateral system covering two-thirds of the global economy.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Coordinated price floors for critical minerals would directly affect commodity pricing and mining economics; a structural anti-China supply-chain play to track.
insights.quote: FORGE is designed as a preferential trade-and-investment zone for critical minerals, using coordinated price floors to counter adversarial market manipulation, primarily from China.
insights.locator: The administration has launched a similarly ambitious effort

themes.theme: Conditional continuity at Bretton Woods and the G20

insights.claim: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent slammed the IMF for straying into climate, gender, and social issues and the World Bank for vapid marketing, demanding a return to fundamentals, and within about a year both institutions largely regained the administration's good graces by accommodating U.S. preferences.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Bretton Woods institutions proved adaptable and survived the threat; expect a continued mission narrowing toward macro surveillance, infrastructure, and energy including fossil and nuclear.
insights.quote: His vision for both institutions was a “return to the fundamentals”
insights.locator: This stance is most apparent with respect to the Bretton Woods

insights.claim: The IMF's latest Article IV for China took a harder line on Beijing's export-driven growth and exchange-rate policies, and Georgieva cut climate mentions in her 2026 Spring Meetings address to once, down from six in 2024.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Quantifiable evidence of IMF rhetorical realignment toward U.S. priorities and away from climate, a tell for how IO leadership accommodates pressure.
insights.quote: the word appearing just once in her 2026 Spring Meetings address, down from six times in 2024.
insights.locator: The International Monetary Fund’s most recent Article IV

insights.claim: World Bank President Ajay Banga accommodated U.S. preferences by dropping the long-standing nuclear-financing ban and joining the Board of Peace, yet the bank quietly held its Climate Change Action Plan, with 48 percent of FY2024-25 financing classified as having climate co-benefits versus a 35 percent commitment.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Nuclear-financing reversal opens multilateral capital to nuclear projects; the gap between self-censorship and unchanged climate lending shows IOs can comply rhetorically while preserving programs.
insights.quote: He also dropped the bank’s long-standing prohibition on nuclear energy financing
insights.locator: At the World Bank, President Ajay Banga

insights.claim: Using its 2025-26 G20 chair the U.S. stripped the agenda of climate, social, and development workstreams in favor of three priorities—deregulation, affordable and secure energy, and AI/emerging-tech innovation—erasing debt-distress, sovereign-debt-restructuring, and inequality agendas elevated by Indonesia, India, Brazil, and South Africa.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Sovereign-debt and development-finance reform lose their main multilateral forum, a setback for distressed-debt and frontier-market borrowers; energy and AI move to the center.
insights.quote: using the United States’ year as chair to strip the agenda of discussions on climate, social issues, and development, in favor of core macroeconomic coordination.
insights.locator: A similar pattern of initial hostility

insights.claim: Ambassador Mike Waltz declared the U.S. will not leave the UN but make the UN great again, defending its standard-setting role in aviation English, spectrum, space, and telecoms and arguing if we didn't invent it, someone else would.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Technical UN bodies governing aviation, spectrum, IP, and nuclear safeguards remain U.S.-engaged, lowering disruption risk to the infrastructure firms depend on daily.
insights.quote: “If we didn’t invent it, someone else would.”
insights.locator: In April 2026, during the first congressional hearings

insights.claim: The administration is reconstructing a leaner, conditional aid architecture after dismantling USAID (with U.S. bilateral ODA falling a record 57 percent in 2025), routing a humanitarian reset through OCHA with $2 billion in December 2025 and $1.8 billion in May 2026, and tying aid to UN voting records.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Aid becomes explicitly transactional and politically conditioned; OCHA broke its pooled-funding model and excluded Afghanistan, Yemen, and Somalia, reshaping where humanitarian capital flows.
insights.quote: Individual UN agencies will need to adapt, shrink, or die.
insights.locator: In what has been named the “humanitarian reset,”

insights.claim: OCHA head Tom Fletcher said in early May he would sooner forgo American funding than accept conditions tied to abortion, gender identity, and DEI under the administration's expanded Mexico City Policy, leaving the $1.8 billion pledge's fate unclear.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Ideological conditions can trigger IO refusal even of large U.S. pledges, a source of funding uncertainty for humanitarian operations.
insights.quote: he would sooner forgo American funding than accept conditions tied to abortion, gender identity, and diversity, equity, and inclusion
insights.locator: Still, the fate of the most recent $1.8 billion

insights.claim: The authors argue the most enduring damage is reputational, as repeated U.S. reversals across trade, security, climate, development, and health convince allies and the Global South that U.S. commitments are reversible, accelerating hedging and openness to Chinese or alternative institutional partnerships.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Credibility erosion is the durable cost: expect persistent allied diversification and de-risking from U.S. dependence, a structural headwind for U.S. coalition leverage.
insights.quote: a growing perception that U.S. commitments are less credible, heavily subject to domestic political swings, and thus reversible over the short and medium term.
insights.locator: Perhaps the most enduring damage of U.S. defection

insights.claim: The authors warn that every institution the U.S. retreats from creates openings for China, which through efforts like its September 2025 Global Governance Initiative is cultivating an image as a steady, predictable responsible stakeholder, accelerating the emergence of a UN with Chinese characteristics.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: U.S. withdrawal is a direct transfer of agenda-setting power to Beijing, especially on human rights and standards; position for rising Chinese influence in IO governance.
insights.quote: Such trends are likely to accelerate the emergence of “a UN with Chinese characteristics.”
insights.locator: Just as importantly, vacuums in international governance

insights.claim: The authors call it a profound irony that an administration preoccupied with restoring American strength may produce one of the most significant long-term erosions of U.S. influence in the postwar era, not via a single failure but through accumulated unpredictability and institutional abandonment.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: The core thesis: short-term leverage is being traded for long-term decline in U.S. structural power, a slow-burn risk to dollar-system and alliance durability.
insights.quote: may ultimately contribute to one of the most significant long-term erosions of American influence in the postwar era
insights.locator: An administration deeply preoccupied with restoring

insights.claim: The authors offer a contrarian silver lining that only an administration willing to provoke institutional upheaval could force the serious reform conversations now occurring across the multilateral system and within NATO.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: Disruption could yield a leaner, more disciplined institutional layer; the bull case is genuine reform if the U.S. pairs pressure with coalition-building.
insights.quote: only an administration willing to generate confrontation and provoke institutional upheaval could force serious reform conversations to occur at all.
insights.locator: For liberal internationalists, this raises a possibility

insights.claim: The U.S. funded the WHO at about $1.284 billion a year before withdrawal—less than what Americans spend annually on artichokes—which the authors cite as evidence the exit decision was not based on rigorous cost-benefit analysis.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A vivid scale check showing trivial fiscal savings against large strategic costs, reinforcing that ideological withdrawals can be economically irrational and possibly reversible.
insights.quote: less than what Americans spend annually on artichokes.
insights.locator: The U.S. departure from the WHO is a case in point.

insights.claim: The authors lay out three darker scenarios: hollowed-out zombie institutions formally intact but instrumentalized by great powers, an order progressively shaped by Chinese preferences, or a fragmented landscape where middle powers fill gaps but lack scale and legitimacy for global coordination.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: A scenario map for global governance risk; each path implies different exposure to coordination failure, Chinese rule-setting, or regional fragmentation.
insights.quote: a world of increasingly hollowed-out “zombie” institutions: formally intact, but substantively weakened, underfunded, and instrumentalized by great powers.
insights.locator: There are also darker possibilities.

insights.claim: The authors argue durable reform requires coalition-building, legitimacy, and strategic discipline rather than unilateral coercion, warning that reforms imposed without buy-in are less likely to survive political transitions.
insights.speaker: Gustavo Romero
insights.so_what: The test for whether U.S. pressure yields lasting reform or just reversible damage is allied coordination; its current absence is the key bearish tell.
insights.quote: An enduring challenge for the United States is to press for multilateral institutional reform without simultaneously destroying the very U.S. influence that makes reform possible.
insights.locator: But successful reform requires coalition-building
